ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for low fat chocolate milk; SNOMED CT coverage for flavoured dairy beverages is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of low fat chocolate milk is one glass of approximately 200 ml (about 207 g), commonly consumed as a snack or post-exercise recovery drink.

nutrient_source.title: Low Fat Chocolate Milk — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for low fat chocolate milk (cr_id F01030008). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Chocolate milk, low-fat — Glycemic Index entry
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 40 (low GI) for low fat chocolate milk is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database data for chocolate milk. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 11.6 × 207/100) / 100 ≈ 9.5 per 207 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (10.5 g/100 g) with minimal dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g); sodium is low at 25 mg/100 g but added sugar independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit low fat chocolate milk due to its high added sugar content (10.5 g/100 g), which independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, and should prefer plain unsweetened low fat milk instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (10.5 g/100 g) with very low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g); liquid form accelerates glucose absorption, increasing postprandial blood glucose spike risk despite a low GI of 40.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit low fat chocolate milk because its high added sugar load (10.5 g/100 g) and negligible fibre (0.3 g/100 g) can cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation; plain unsweetened low fat milk is a safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.9 g/100 g) and low sodium (25 mg/100 g) are favourable, but high added sugar (10.5 g/100 g) raises triglyceride and cardiovascular risk at regular consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, low fat chocolate milk's low saturated fat and sodium profile is partially favourable, but its high added sugar content (10.5 g/100 g) should prompt limiting intake and choosing plain low fat milk to reduce sugar-driven triglyceride elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Calcium (78 mg/100 g) and protein (4.4 g/100 g) support bone health relevant to RA patients on corticosteroids; however, high added sugar (10.5 g/100 g) may promote low-grade inflammation at high intake.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Low fat chocolate milk provides useful calcium and protein for rheumatoid arthritis patients, but its high added sugar content (10.5 g/100 g) may promote low-grade inflammation; prefer plain low fat milk or unsweetened cocoa milk to reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low fat chocolate milk is a processed dairy beverage made from pasteurised low fat cow's milk flavoured with cocoa or chocolate syrup and sweetened with added sugar. It has a smooth, mildly sweet chocolate flavour with a light, thin body compared to full-fat chocolate milk. Per 100 g it provides 77 kcal, 4.4 g protein, 1.4 g fat (of which approximately 0.9 g is saturated), 11.6 g carbohydrate, 10.5 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, 25 mg sodium, 78 mg calcium, and 0.2 mg iron. The glycemic index is approximately 40 (low GI), but the high added sugar content (10.5 g/100 g) and minimal fibre mean that liquid glucose absorption is rapid, making it a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. The calcium and protein content make it a useful post-exercise recovery drink and a source of bone-supporting nutrients, particularly relevant for rheumatoid arthritis patients on corticosteroids. Cocoa polyphenols contribute modest antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Despite its low GI, the American Heart Association 2018 guidelines flag high added sugar intake as an independent cardiovascular and hypertension risk factor. It is widely consumed as a snack or children's beverage and is available commercially in Singapore and globally. Suitable for vegetarians; contains milk allergen; not suitable for vegans or lactose-intolerant individuals.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01030008) with confidence 0.95. GI value of 40 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database data for low fat chocolate milk. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present and supported.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for low fat chocolate milk not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients and water content are not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this food item. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: trans fat is confirmed absent in low fat dairy milk products per standard dairy composition data. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.9 g/100 g based on typical low fat milk fat composition (~65% saturated of total fat 1.4 g); HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 207 g serving (200 ml glass): GL = (40 × 11.6 × 2.07) / 100 ≈ 9.6, rounded to 9.5. This food is a generic dairy beverage record; no Singaporean-specific cuisine multilingual naming is required as it is not a Singaporean dish. Cuisine is classified as Generic/Western.
